steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank follows 'was a' and must be filled with 'supernova' plus punctuation that correctly links it to the defining phrase 'the explosion of a massive star, which blasts large amounts of radiation, heavy elements, and debris into space.' That phrase is an appositive — it renames and defines 'supernova.'

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'supernova, the': a comma before an appositive is acceptable only when the appositive is non-restrictive and set off with a matching comma at the end — but the closing dash in the sentence makes a comma here create mismatched punctuation and a structural error. ✗
  • Choice B — 'supernova. The': a period splits the sentence into two, leaving 'The explosion of a massive star…that was first detected by American astronomer Josef Johnson' as a fragment without a main verb. ✗
  • Choice C — 'supernova—the': an em dash opens an appositive phrase, which is then closed by the em dash already in the sentence before 'that was first detected.' The dash pair correctly sets off the entire defining phrase. ✓
  • Choice D — 'supernova: the': a colon can introduce an explanation, but the sentence already has a dash later; mixing a colon and a closing dash to frame an appositive is not standard, and a colon is not the right tool for a noun appositive mid-sentence. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
